space.name: Engineering
space.description: How Muster is built and how it runs — architecture, the human/agent loop, deploy notes, and lessons from shipping the system with the system.

pages.title: Muster architecture — four planes around a task board
pages.summary: The whole system is four cooperating planes with one shared spine: the os_tasks record.
pages.body:
# Muster architecture — four planes around a task board

Muster is not an admin panel. It's a **loop**: a human and an autonomous agent fleet coordinating through **one task record** (`os_tasks`), under a written constitution, grounded by a local RAG knowledge base, remembering across sessions on disk.

The system is four cooperating planes, all hanging off one spine.

## The spine — the CMS (`os_*` schema)

A self-hosted Directus over Postgres. The `os_tasks` collection is the single record of work; everything else (projects, sprints, deals, invoices, releases, KB) is the agency operating system around it. Schema is snapshot-driven and pruned to a leak-free, sellable core (**141 → 50 collections**).

## Plane 1 — the CMS / task substrate

Projects, sprints, and tasks. Both humans (portal) and agents (MCP bridge) read and write the **same rows**. A task moved to `completed` by an agent looks identical to one a human closed.

## Plane 2 — the portal (human surface)

A containerized Next.js app rendering the board, CRM, billing, releases, and the Knowledge Base. This is what you're reading this page in. It is generic-branded per profile so the sellable template never says “Analog Elk.”

## Plane 3 — the RAG knowledge engine

A bundled local retrieval stack (Postgres + Qdrant + Redis + a RAG API) that grounds the agents in real docs instead of guesses. Runs on an isolated port so it never collides with another instance on the same box.

## Plane 4 — Claude-OS (the agent governance layer)

A written **constitution**, an **MCP** bridge to the CMS, lifecycle **hooks**, and on-disk **memory**. This is what turns a stateless model into a fleet that can pick up work, act, and hand off across sessions.

## Why it holds together

Every plane touches the same `os_tasks` row. The human sees it in the portal; the agent sees it over MCP; the KB grounds what the agent does to it; the constitution governs how. One record, four planes, one loop.

pages.title: How the human/agent loop closes
pages.summary: The interesting claim isn't the panel — it's the loop. Here is exactly how it closes across sessions.
pages.body:
# How the human/agent loop closes

The interesting claim of Muster is not the admin panel — it's the **loop**: a human and an autonomous agent fleet coordinating through one task record, under a written constitution, grounded by a local RAG KB, remembering across sessions on disk.

## The cycle

1. **Human sets intent** — a task lands in `os_tasks` (from the portal, or a one-line ask). One fork at a time; decisions are reached with the human, not around them.
2. **Agents ground themselves** — before acting, the fleet queries the local RAG KB for real guidance (e.g. Directus snapshot/migration patterns) instead of hallucinating.
3. **Fan-out** — the parallel-execution engine (Max Mode) splits the work across many agents, each owning a slice.
4. **Adversarial verify** — separate skeptic agents try to *prove* the work is wrong (e.g. that real client data leaked into the sellable template). Nothing ships until they fail to break it.
5. **From-scratch loop-proof** — a clean stack is booted and the change is exercised end to end (migrate → seed → read a task back through the API). This catches bugs offline mocks cannot.
6. **The record updates** — the task moves to `completed`; memory is written to disk; the next session picks up from the record, not from chat history.

## Why disk memory matters

LLMs are stateless. The loop only closes because state lives in two durable places: the **`os_tasks` record** (what to do / what's done) and **on-disk memory** (what was learned). A fresh session re-onboards from both and continues — the loop survives the context window.

## The recursion

Muster was built **by** this loop: the system used its own fan-out engine to build its own installer, its own adversarial verifiers to check its own template for leaks, and its own from-scratch loop-proof to catch bugs in its own packaging. Building it was the loop's final exam.

pages.title: Gotchas & lessons from the build
pages.summary: The real bugs the from-scratch loop-proof caught that offline mocks missed — and the lessons.
pages.body:
# Gotchas & lessons from the build

These are the concrete bugs the **from-scratch loop-proof** caught during the P2 build (the `os_*` schema + seeds), which the agents' offline mocks could not.

## 1. Port collision fooled the health check

The bundled RAG API defaulted to port **9100** — which collided with an elk-v3 engine already running on the box. Worse, `doctor` was *fooled* into reporting that other engine healthy. **Fix:** default the bundled engine to **19100**, and make `doctor` confirm it's talking to *our* engine on the port, not just any listener.

> Lesson: a health check that only pings a port can be satisfied by the wrong process. Verify identity, not just liveness.

## 2. `releases` seed missing required `repository_id`

A `release` belongs to a repository (which carries the project). The seed omitted the required `repository_id`, so seeding failed only on a clean install. One profile was even missing its `repositories.json` entirely. **Fix:** add the repo rows and wire `repository_id` in both profiles.

> Lesson: required foreign keys are invisible until you seed into an empty database. Test the empty case.

## 3. Bare-named seed files hit the wrong collection

Seed files named `projects.json` collided with a kept `projects` UI-folder collection, so the loader seeded the **wrong target**. **Fix:** namespace seed files as `os_*.json` and make the loader prefer real table collections over UI-folder aliases.

> Lesson: name collisions between data tables and UI-only folders are silent and destructive. Namespace everything.

## Meta-lesson

All three bugs share a root cause: **offline mocks lie**. Only booting a clean stack and reading a real row back through the real API surfaced them. The loop-proof — migrate, seed, read-back — is now a required gate, not a nicety.

pages.title: Self-hosting Muster
pages.summary: One command stands up the whole substrate. What runs, and how it stays leak-free and reproducible.
pages.body:
# Self-hosting Muster

The human/agent task substrate stands up from **one command**. This page is the operator's orientation.

## What runs

- **Postgres** — the database under Directus.
- **Directus** (`os_*` schema) — the CMS spine and task record.
- **Portal** — the containerized Next.js human surface, behind Caddy with automatic TLS (musterr.dev hostnames on the live demo box).
- **RAG engine** — the bundled local knowledge stack (isolated port so it never collides with another instance).

## Reproducibility

Schema is applied from a **snapshot**, not by hand. Seed data ships as versioned `os_*.json` profiles (a generic “Demo Co” profile and an Analog Elk profile). `migrate` then `seed` then a read-back through the API is the green gate. A `doctor` command checks every service and confirms it's talking to *our* engine, not a stray listener.

## Staying sellable / leak-free

The schema was pruned **141 → 50 collections** and three adversarial verifier agents tried to prove real client data leaked into the template — all returned `leakProven: false`. Branding is generic per profile so the template never hard-codes “Analog Elk.”

## The live demo

A public, **read-only** demo runs the whole stack: a non-admin `Employee` role carrying a read-only policy (create/update/delete return 403), so anyone can explore the real board without being able to change it. Four self-host blockers were fixed to get there: subdomain redirects, cookie scoping, test-data visibility, and portal branding.

pages.title: The whitepaper
pages.summary: The narrative log of the build — the thesis, the eight phases, and the recursion, session by session.
pages.body:
# The whitepaper

This is the short form. The full build log is the front door of the Muster site itself — the homepage *is* the whitepaper.

## The thesis this project proves

The interesting claim of the Analog Elk system is not the admin panel — it's the **loop**: a human and an autonomous agent fleet coordinating through one task record (`os_tasks`), under a written constitution, grounded by a local RAG KB, remembering across sessions on disk. Muster is the loop, packaged. Building it was the loop's final exam: **the system used its own CMS, agents, and governance to produce the thing that ships the system.**

## The eight phases (all green)

- **P0** — scaffold: repo, CLI skeleton, secrets/profile layer, `doctor` framework.
- **P1** — compose core: Postgres + Directus up green locally.
- **P2** — `os_*` schema snapshot + seed profiles — **built by Max Mode** (the system's own fan-out engine), leak-verified by adversarial agents.
- **P3** — the bundled RAG knowledge engine.
- **P4** — the portal: the human surface, containerized.
- **P5** — Claude-OS wiring: constitution + MCP + hooks + memory. The loop closes on a from-scratch install (the capstone).
- **P6** — cloud deploy + the live demo URL.
- **P7** — self-host packaging: deploy blueprints, published images, README/landing.

## The recursion in one line

The system used its fan-out engine to build its own installer, its adversarial verifiers to check its own template for leaks, and its from-scratch loop-proof to catch bugs in its own packaging — including one where its own health check was fooled by its own other instance.

## Read the full log

The complete, session-by-session narrative lives in `WHITEPAPER-LOG.md` in the repository and is rendered as the public Muster homepage.
